elements.sent-2411-001: The evolution of vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication brings significant challenges, such as data integrity and vulnerabilities stemming from centralized management.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper presents an innovative integration of decentralized blockchain technology with V2X communication through a multi-layered architecture that combines the Simulation of Urban Mobility (SUMO) traffic simulator and the BlockSim blockchain simulator.
elements.sent-2411-003: In addition, as the Social Internet of Vehicles (SIoV) emerges, efficient resource management becomes indispensable for ensuring seamless communication.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also propose a reference multi-connectivity management method named Enhanced MAX-SINR, designed to advance research in blockchain-specific approaches, taking into account retransmission successfull rates.
elements.sent-2411-005: We evaluate blockchain performance in diverse environments such as urban, suburban, and rural areas, demonstrating that enhancing the success rate of retransmitted blockchain-related messages significantly boosts blockchain transaction performance and provides a foundation for developing intelligent SIoV systems.
elements.sent-2411-006: The rapid evolution of communication technology has significantly accelerated research in vehicular network communication.
elements.sent-2411-007: A major milestone in this field is vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication, which establishes a cellular standard enhancing connectivity between vehicles and various entities\cite{noor20226g .}
elements.sent-2411-008: The Social Internet of Vehicles (SIoV) improves V2X networks by facilitating the formation of social network-like connections among vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-009: This connectivity enables vehicles to dynamically exchange crucial information on safety, efficiency, and comfort, enhancing real-time decision-making in V2X applications.\cite{Alam2015,liu2022vrepchain .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Blockchain technology can be adapted to improve trust, data integrity, and privacy in V2X systems\cite{rao2023blockchain .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The decentralized nature of blockchain effectively mitigates single points of attack and failure while utilizing consensus mechanisms to verify the security of network participants and data~\cite{alladi2022comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Building on these advantages, Liu et al.~\cite{liu2022vrepchain introduced a blockchain-based reputation system for SIoV, designed to block the dissemination of fraudulent vehicle reputation scores among vehicles and to safeguard the privacy of these scores.}
elements.sent-2411-013: With the introduction of sidelink communication and satellite integration, transmission reliability is enhanced through sophisticated multi-connectivity management\cite{annu2024,liang2019spectrum .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Ji et al.\cite{ji2023multi enhanced this by integrating deep reinforcement learning, which elevates spectrum utilization and adaptability to dynamic conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Chen et al.\cite{ipcsocial applied role-oriented strategies in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to optimize SIoV communication efficiency, focusing on resource management tailored to vehicle social roles.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Therefore, an environment where a blockchain-based application and a V2X network operate on a system with multi-connectivity deserves further study to deliver optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-017: In several studies, blockchain and V2X networks have been integrated and simulated through various approaches.
elements.sent-2411-018: Tu et al.\cite{tu2023secure employed a custom simulation that allowed adjustable parameters such as transmission distance, vehicle speed, and blockchain configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Liu et al.\cite{liu2022vrepchain utilized NetLogo primarily to simulate dynamic traffic scenarios, where vehicles were modeled as blockchain nodes with basic behaviors.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Chavhan et al. ~\cite{chavhan2023edge adopted the Simulation of Urban Mobility (SUMO) for simulating vehicular platoon networks to handle traffic data, while NetSim was used to simulate blockchain, emphasizing network layer activities.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Nonetheless, these studies neglect to simulate a fully integrated environment that includes vehicular traffic, various connectivity options, and authentic blockchain mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-022: Performance analysis on blockchain is an increasingly popular topic.
elements.sent-2411-023: Gupta et al.\cite{gupta2023performance compared consensus mechanisms in permissioned blockchains to assess their throughput, latency, and scalability under different loads.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Fan et al.\cite{fan2022performance examined transaction throughput in static nodes, adjusting blockchain parameters for testing.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Kim\cite{kim2019impacts studied the effect of node mobility on blockchain performance in V2X networks.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Gao et al.\cite{gao2021multi analyzed blockchain performance under various vehicle densities, emphasizing that transaction throughput depends on successful data transmission.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Fardad et al.\cite{fardad2024blockchain investigated blockchain performance with different connectivity options under diverse traffic loads.}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, these studies have not thoroughly examined the multi-connectivity transmission mechanisms of future vehicular networks, such as the impact of satellite connections and other innovative approaches on blockchain performance.
elements.sent-2411-029: To effectively conduct this research, we introduce a novel approach combining satellite-assisted V2X communication with blockchain technology and advanced simulation tools.
elements.sent-2411-030: This multi-layer system facilitates realistic testing and optimization of V2X networks.
elements.sent-2411-031: Leveraging the SUMO traffic simulator and the BlockSim blockchain simulator\cite{alharby2020blocksim , alongside reference multi-connectivity management strategies, we evaluate blockchain performance in diverse environments such as urban, suburban, and rural areas.}
elements.sent-2411-033: We create a comprehensive multi-layer simulator that accurately models aspects from vehicle data generation to blockchain functionality, providing a solid foundation for future research.
elements.sent-2411-034: The implementation challenges at both the layer and system levels have been addressed to fulfill the requirements for a broad spectrum of functions.
elements.sent-2411-035: We propose a reference multi-connectivity management method that focuses on enhancing the success rate of retransmitted blockchain-related messages.
elements.sent-2411-036: This improvement significantly boosts blockchain transaction performance and provides a foundation for developing intelligent systems.
elements.sent-2411-037: The evaluation of the system architecture across various geographical regions demonstrated a significant improvement.
elements.sent-2411-038: By implementing retransmission enhancements, there was an 18.71\% increase in blockchain transaction throughput, compared to the MARL-based approach that primarily targeted non-blockchain performance objectives.
elements.sent-2411-039: The source code of the multi-layer blockchain V2X system is also released to facilitate verification and future development\footnote{The source codes can be found at \url{https://github.com/IPCLab/V2XBlockchain.git .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The paper is organized as follows: Section~\ref{sec:imple_arch describes the V2X and blockchain-integrated system architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Section~\ref{sec:bc_mechanism discusses the blockchain mechanism and implementation challenges in V2X.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Section~\ref{sec:connectivity covers the Multi-Connectivity Management method.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Section~\ref{sec:results presents simulation results and performance evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Finally, Section~\ref{sec:conclusion concludes the study and suggests future directions.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Figure~\ref{fig:Imple_Arch depicts the multi-layer integration of blockchain technology with a satellite-assisted SIoV network, highlighting the key features of each layer.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{System_for_Blockchain_and_V2X_Communication_Integration.pdf} \caption{The the multi-layer model for integrating blockchain and SIoV networks.} \label{fig:Imple_Arch}
elements.sent-2411-046: This layer allows customizable vehicle datasets, like those generated by SUMO, for adaptable simulations.
elements.sent-2411-047: Traffic data is categorized into urban, suburban, and rural densities, with the optional use of satellite data to enhance V2X connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-048: Road-side units (RSUs) are strategically positioned to facilitate flexible communication and function as blockchain nodes.
elements.sent-2411-049: The Connection Layer enables V2X communication, facilitating interactions among vehicles, infrastructure, and satellites.
elements.sent-2411-050: SIoV enhances this by creating dynamic networks for collaborative sharing of safety and traffic data, optimizing communication and resource allocation.
elements.sent-2411-051: It ensures secure message transmission across V2X types and verifies each message before packaging for blockchain transactions, enhancing data integrity and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-052: Vehicles in the environment are denoted by the set \(\mathbf{V = \{1, 2, \dots, v\}\), where \(v\) is the total number of vehicles.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The SINR \(\gamma_{v, k}\) for a vehicle is given by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:sinr} \gamma_{v, k} = \frac{PR_{v, k}}{N_0^2 + I_{v, k}^{i-1}}\end{equation} where \(PR_{v, k}\) is the received signal power from vehicle \(v\) on sub-channel \(k\), \(N_0^2\) is the noise variance, and \(I_{v,k}\) is the co-channel interference power from other vehicles on the same sub-channel.
elements.sent-2411-054: Each vehicle employs a multi-connectivity management approach to choose its transmission settings.
elements.sent-2411-055: The Consensus Layer utilizes BlockSim\cite{alharby2020blocksim to simulate a blockchain environment.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In this setup, RSUs function as nodes that package transactions, generate blocks, and handle broadcasting and synchronization.
elements.sent-2411-057: They compete to create blocks based on their hash power, indicating their computational capability.
elements.sent-2411-058: Detailed operational mechanisms of the blockchain will be covered in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-059: In this framework, RSUs function as blockchain nodes, each equipped with a node ID, hash power, and a local blockchain initialized by a genesis block.
elements.sent-2411-060: They manage a transaction pool, converting messages into transactions and appending them to the blockchain.
elements.sent-2411-061: The following sections detail primary functions customized for transaction creation and block generation/synchronization in V2X networks.
elements.sent-2411-062: Figure~\ref{fig:tx_create demonstrates the process of message transmission and transaction verification.}
elements.sent-2411-063: There are three paths of transmission: vehicle-to-vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2V2I), which involves an RSU in our setup, vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I), and vehicle-to-satellite-to-infrastructure (V2S2I).
elements.sent-2411-064: These diverse paths ensure that messages can reach a blockchain node even in the absence of a nearby RSU.
elements.sent-2411-065: The message latency $L_M$ is defined as the time it takes for a message to travel from the vehicle to the RSU.
elements.sent-2411-066: Upon receiving a message, the RSU verifies its integrity and then encapsulates it as a transaction for further processing within the blockchain network.
elements.sent-2411-067: Furthermore, a Gaussian distribution is used to allocate the hash power for each RSU miner.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Transaction_Creation_ver1.pdf} \caption{Message transmission and transaction creation in V2X networks.} \label{fig:tx_create}
elements.sent-2411-068: The block generation process is illustrated in \figurename~\ref{fig:block_generate .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Throughout the simulation, miner nodes actively engage in the consensus mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-070: They compete to generate blocks, with the fastest miner considered the winner.
elements.sent-2411-071: The winning node selects transactions from the pool to add to its local blockchain.
elements.sent-2411-072: After confirming the block is stored locally, the miner re-enters the consensus process for the next round.
elements.sent-2411-073: The block generation time $t_G$ is modeled by an exponential distribution: \begin{equation}t_G \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda = R_n \times \frac{1}{T_{G}} )\end{equation} where ${R_n}$ denotes the hash power value $H_n$ of the miner node $n$ as a proportion of all miner nodes.
elements.sent-2411-074: The block broadcast process, essential for network synchronization, is shown in green in \figurename~\ref{fig:timeline .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Miners broadcast the generated block across the network, with the receiving time of other nodes $t_R$ following an exponential distribution: \begin{equation}t_R \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda = \frac{1}{T_{R}} )\end{equation} where ${t_R}$ is a constant, represents the average time for a node to receive the block.
elements.sent-2411-076: Once a receiving node gets the broadcasted block, it will validate the block.
elements.sent-2411-077: If the block’s Hash Value~\cite{meijers2022blockchain , which uniquely links each block to its predecessor, doesn’t match the last block in the receiving node’s blockchain, the block is discarded to ensure continuity and data integrity.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Integrating blockchain with V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) systems presents challenges.
elements.sent-2411-079: Layer-specific issues can be addressed within individual layers, whereas system-wide challenges affect the whole system.
elements.sent-2411-080: To balance high data rates with security and decentralization in V2X and blockchain integration, propose that vehicles upload only essential Key Messages—vehicle ID, speed, and location—to reduce bandwidth and storage.
elements.sent-2411-081: Introduce a verification method between the Connection and Consensus Layers, storing only fully transmitted messages in the transaction pool to ensure integrity.
elements.sent-2411-082: Simulating parallel multitasking poses a significant challenge in system integration.
elements.sent-2411-083: During the implementation of the three-layer architecture shown in \figurename~\ref{fig:Imple_Arch , it was observed that the system operates on two timelines as illustrated in Figure \figurename~\ref{fig:timeline .}}
elements.sent-2411-084: This necessitates the use of a global clock for synchronization.
elements.sent-2411-085: Message latency to the RSU is determined by the connection mode, affecting simulation time.
elements.sent-2411-086: When the global clock matches the simulated arrival time at the RSU, the message is verified and added to the transaction pool.
elements.sent-2411-087: Similarly, at the designated block generation time, miners bundle transactions into a block, broadcasting it to receiving nodes as the clock aligns with the reception time.
elements.sent-2411-088: The global clock maintains the correct event execution sequence.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{timeline2.pdf} \caption{Integrated System for Parallel Processing of Two Timelines. The blue and green sections are two timelines respectively.} \label{fig:timeline}
elements.sent-2411-089: We introduce an improved multi-connectivity management technique named Enhanced MAX-SINR as reference, designed to advance research in blockchain-specific approaches.
elements.sent-2411-090: This method builds upon the conventional MAX-SINR technique.
elements.sent-2411-091: Given that message retransmission poses a significant challenge to blockchain transaction throughput\cite{fardad2024blockchain , Enhanced MAX-SINR dynamically switches channels and connections to mitigate congestion, thus expediting message delivery forming successful transactions.}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Enhanced MAX-SINR Selection Method} \label{alg:E-MAXSINR} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE $i$: the message transmitted for the $i$-th time; $\mathbf{V}$: the set of each vehicle $v$ in the current environment; ${\mathbf{A}_v}^{(i-1)}$: the set of previous transmission selections for each vehicle $v$, including connection mode $M_v^{(i-1)}$, transmission signal power $PT_v^{(i-1)}$, sub-channel $k_v^{(i-1)}$, and transmission success or failure ${SF}_v^{(i-1)}$; \ENSURE $\mathcal{A}^{i}$: the set of transmission selections for the $i$-th transmission of all vehicles \STATE Each vehicle selects an action for this transmission \FOR{each vehicle $v \in \mathbf{V}$} \IF{$i = 0$} \STATE ${\mathbf{A}_v}^{i} \gets$ MAX-SINR selection method \ELSIF{$SF_v^{(i-1)} = \text{Failure}$} \STATE $strategy = \text{random integer in } \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ \STATE switch $strategy$ \STATE case 0: \begin{minipage}{0.77\linewidth} Select ${\mathbf{A}_v}^{i}$ with the maximum SINR in a connection mode other than $M_v^{(i-1)}$ \end{minipage} \STATE case 1: \begin{minipage}{0.77\linewidth} Select ${\mathbf{A}_v}^{i}$ with the maximum SINR in the same connection mode, but with a sub-channel other than $k_v^{(i-1)}$ \end{minipage} \STATE case 2: \begin{minipage}{0.77\linewidth} ${\mathbf{A}_v}^{i} \gets {\mathbf{A}_v}^{(i-1)}$ \end{minipage} \STATE case 3: \begin{minipage}{0.77\linewidth} ${\mathbf{A}_v}^{i} \gets$ MAX-SINR selection method \end{minipage} \STATE end switch \ENDIF \ENDFOR \RETURN $\mathcal{A}^{i}$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-092: The action determined by the vehicle $v$ as a smart agent is denoted by the set $A_v$ and the collective actions of all vehicles are represented by $\mathcal{A}$ : \begin{equation}A_v = \{M_v, PT_v, k_v\}, \quad \mathcal{A} = \{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_v\}\end{equation} where \( M_v \) denotes the chosen connection mode, \( PT_v \) is the transmission signal power, and \( k_v \) refers to the sub-channel.
elements.sent-2411-093: When the MAX-SINR method attempts a retransmission after a failure, it considers only interference from the previous transmission to calculate SINR, limiting the vehicle's ability to avoid congested channels and risking further failures.
elements.sent-2411-094: To address this, The Enhanced MAX-SINR method in Algorithm~\ref{alg:E-MAXSINR , offering four additional selection strategies for vehicles, based on comparison with the previous transmission:.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Change Connection Mode: Select a channel with the maximum SINR under a different connection mode.
elements.sent-2411-096: Change Sub-channel: Select a different sub-channel with the maximum SINR under the same connection mode.
elements.sent-2411-097: Keep transmission selection: Use the same action.
elements.sent-2411-098: Reapply MAX-SINR: Reapply the MAX-SINR method to determine a new action.
elements.sent-2411-099: We use SUMO to simulate traffic flows in the Yujing District of Tainan City.
elements.sent-2411-100: The area offers a diverse mix of urban, suburban, and rural regions, with vehicle densities of over 2000, 1000, and 400 vehicles per km respectively, according to Taiwan’s Ministry of Transportation.
elements.sent-2411-101: In a 50-second simulation using SUMO, average vehicle counts of 100 to 500 result in densities of approximately 8.47 to 42.34 vehicles per km² across a map of $8.7 \times 11.7$ km².
elements.sent-2411-102: The actual road network spans 11.81 km².
elements.sent-2411-103: Satellite data from CelesTrak\cite{celestrak_starlink_data enhances the V2X environment, and RSU deployment is shown in \figurename~\ref{fig:map .}}
elements.sent-2411-104: Details on simulation parameters are provided in Table \ref{table:sim_parameters .}
elements.sent-2411-105: The simulation runs on a PC with an Intel Core i7-13700 CPU and 32 GB memory.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{Vehicle_and_RSU_Distribution_in_Yujing_District_Simulation.pdf} \caption{Vehicles and RSUs Distribution Generated by SUMO Simulation. } \label{fig:map}
elements.figure-2411-006: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \caption{Simulation Parameters} \label{table:sim_parameters} 0.8{ \begin{tabular}{|p{4.5cm}|p{5.5cm}|} Parameter Value Area (km$^2$) 8.7 $\times$ 11.7 RSUs(Miner; Regular) 54(30; 24) RSU Coverage Distance (m) 500 RAU Placement Distance (m) urban: 450; suburban: 750; rural: 1000 Carrier Frequency (GHz) 3.5 Antenna (Height, Gain) Vehicle: 1.5 m, 3 dBi; BS: 25 m, 8 dBi Noise Figure (dB) Vehicle: 9; BS: 5; Satellite: 1.2 Satellite (Orbit, Altitude, Band) LEO, 550 km, Ka (30 GHz) Sub-channels (MHz) V2V\&V2I: $5 \times 1$; V2S: $10 \times 20$ Message Payload Size (bytes) 650 Satellite Tx/Rx Gain 43.2 dBm / 30.5 dBi Transmit Power (dBm) V2V: [23, 10, 15, 17]; V2I: 23; V2S: 33.5 Thermal Noise Floor (dBm) -174 Scintillation Loss (dB) 2.2 Fading Shadowing: Log-normal; Fast: Rayleigh Pathloss Model V2I,V2V: TR 38.886~\cite{3gpp38.886} ; V2S: TR 38.821~\cite{TR38.821} $simTime$: Simulation Time (s) 50 Key Message Generation Period (s) 0.5 Maximum Transmission Delay (ms) 3 Hash Power Distribution Gaussian Distribution $T_G$: Block Interval (s) 2.7~\cite{son2021effective} $T_R$: Average Block Receive Time (s) 0.25~\cite{son2021effective} ${GL}_B$: Block Gas Limit (s) 30,000,000 \href{https://etherscan.io/}{Etherscan} \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-106: To assess the effectiveness of integrating V2X with blockchain networks, we employ the following performance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-107: Message throughput: This metric measures the system-wide number of messages that are successfully transmitted from vehicles to Roadside Units (RSUs) per millisecond and subsequently become valid transactions. \begin{equation}\text{Message Throughput (M/ms)} = \frac{ME}{\sum {L_M}},\end{equation} where $ME$ represents the total count of messages that were successfully transmitted and validated as transactions.
elements.sent-2411-108: The term $\sum L_M$ indicates the cumulative latency experienced by all messages to enter the transaction pool.
elements.sent-2411-109: Transaction throughput: This metric quantifies the average number of transactions that are uploaded to the blockchain every second, which is crucial for evaluating the performance of blockchain applications\cite{fan2020performance . \begin{equation}\label{eq:tx_through} \text{Transaction Throughput (TX/s)} = \frac{\sum TX}{simTime},\end{equation} where $\sum TX$ denotes the total count of transactions successfully recorded on the blockchain after applying the longest chain principle, and $simTime$ represents the total simulation duration.}
elements.sent-2411-110: The performance evaluation includes MARL\cite{ipcsocial , MAX-SINR, and Random methods, along with the reference multi-connectivity management design Enhanced MAX-SINR.}
elements.sent-2411-111: The MARL approach allows vehicles to optimize a utility function aimed at maximizing performance targets for non-blockchain applications, taking into account throughput and delay constraints.
elements.sent-2411-112: Here, each vehicle functions as an independent agent that selects actions based on its current state, which includes connection mode, transmission power, and sub-channel selection.
elements.sent-2411-113: The MARL method employs a global reward system, aggregating immediate rewards of each vehicle's transmission performance at any given moment.
elements.sent-2411-114: The MAX-SINR method optimizes connection by selecting the mode and sub-channel with maximum SINR, as per \eqref{eq:sinr .}
elements.sent-2411-115: In contrast, the Random method uses a uniform distribution for vehicle transmission selection.
elements.sent-2411-116: Message throughput performance across four multi-connectivity selection methods, as shown in~\figurename~\ref{fig:me_throughput .}
elements.sent-2411-117: While the vehicle numbers rise, all methods exhibit an upward trend in key message generation.
elements.sent-2411-118: Our reference Enhanced MAX-SINR method consistently outperforms others due to its additional selection strategies, which boost successful data transmission rates, especially in congested environments.
elements.sent-2411-119: Notably, in a V2X environment with 500 vehicles, our reference Enhanced MAX-SINR method achieves a message throughput of 135.95 M/Ms. The MAX-SINR method follows with 131.06 M/ms but faces real-time congestion issues, placing it second.
elements.sent-2411-120: The MARL method optimizes channel resource use by balancing throughput, latency, and power, achieving 120.64 M/Ms. It slightly surpasses the Random method, which performs poorly due to ineffective option selection in varying network conditions.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{me_2_new1.pdf} \caption{Message throughput under different average numbers of vehicles } \label{fig:me_throughput}
elements.sent-2411-121: The Enhanced MAX-SINR method consistently outperforms others in terms of transaction throughput, achieving 264.286 TX/s at 300 vehicles—an 18.71\% increase over MARL’s 222.62 TX/s.
elements.sent-2411-122: Though MARL optimizes resource utilization, it is not tailored for blockchain, leading to lower throughput.
elements.sent-2411-123: MAX-SINR also faces channel congestion issues, causing transmission failures, and the Random method performs worst due to a lack of congestion management.
elements.sent-2411-124: As vehicle numbers near 300, throughput caps across methods, revealing blockchain’s transaction limits at high densities and how network conditions affect performance.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{tx_2_new1.pdf} \caption{Transaction throughput under different average numbers of vehicles } \label{fig:tx_throughput}
elements.sent-2411-125: Shorter block times may increase throughput but also raise the risk of chain forks, leading to unconfirmed transactions and reduced throughput.
elements.sent-2411-126: Balancing block generation speed and fork risk is essential, especially under high traffic.
elements.sent-2411-127: Despite these challenges, the Enhanced MAX-SINR method consistently achieves the highest throughput at 272.548 TX/s, showing adaptability across varying block intervals.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{Block_Interval_new.pdf} \caption{Comparison of four methods under different block creation speeds } \label{fig:Block_Interval}
elements.sent-2411-128: The experiments emphasize the need to redesign multi-connectivity selection methods in integrated blockchain and V2X networks to enhance blockchain processing performance, considering the impact of the V2X environment.
elements.sent-2411-129: We presented a multi-layer blockchain simulator for satellite-assisted SIoV scenarios, resolving key implementation issues.
elements.sent-2411-130: Performance evaluations across varying traffic volumes underscore the importance of multi-connectivity management design for optimal blockchain performance.
elements.sent-2411-131: Future advancements could involve developing and testing intelligent management methods using the open-source simulator.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
